file.content:
# MHPCO Claim Office Kata

## Setting

You are building the policy management system for the **Most Honorable
Privileged Claims Office for Magical Risks and Cursed Items** (MHPCO),
an old-fashioned insurance institution that covers magical items —
swords, amulets, staves, potions, and components such as runes and
moonstones. The MHPCO is bureaucratic, slightly stingy, and proud of
its venerable traditions.

## Feature

The system supports two operations: **quote** computes the premium for
a list of items a customer wishes to insure, and **claim** processes a
damage report against an existing policy.

## Rules

### Item values and base premiums

Each main item has an insurance value and a base premium according to
the MHPCO price list:

- Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
- Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
- Staff: 800 G / 80 G
- Potion: 400 G / 40 G

Components — for example runes and moonstones — are insured at 250 G
each, with a base premium of 25 G per component. A building block of
3 alike components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G.

### Premium modifiers

- Cursed items add a 50 % risk surcharge.
- Highly enchanted items (enchantment level ≥ 5) add a 30 % risk
  surcharge.
- Long-standing customers (≥ 2 years of business with MHPCO) receive a
  20 % loyalty discount.
- A first insurance carries a 10 % initial assessment surcharge.
- Customers receive a 15 % discount on each contract after their first.
- A 5 G processing fee is added to every premium.

All amounts are rounded to whole G in the MHPCO's favor.

### Claim processing

A deductible of 100 G applies per damage event. The total payout per
policy is capped at twice the insurance sum.

- Damage to items with enchantment level ≥ 8 is reimbursed at 50 % of
  the damage amount.
- Damage to items made of dragon material is fully reimbursed.

## Examples

The examples below illustrate single rules in isolation. Values shown
are base premiums or raw payout amounts before integration with other
modifiers, unless stated otherwise.

### Building block of 3 alike components

- 2 runes → 50 G base premium
- 3 runes → 60 G base premium (block applies)
- 4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block — block requires exactly 3)
- 7 runes → 175 G base premium

### "Alike" components

> ❓ Hmm — "alike" components: does that mean exactly the same type,
> or just the same family (rune-y, gemstone-y)?

- 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G base premium (no block: different types)
- 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G base premium (two separate blocks)

### Modifier scope on multi-item policies

> ❓ A policy with several items: does the cursed surcharge apply to
> the whole policy, or just to the cursed item?

- a policy covers a cursed sword (base premium 100 G) and a plain
  amulet (base premium 60 G) → policy base premium 160 G; the cursed
  surcharge adds 50 G (50 % of the cursed sword's base premium, not
  of the policy total) → 210 G before further modifiers and fee
- item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the
  base premium of the affected item; policy-wide modifiers (loyalty,
  first insurance, follow-up contract) apply to the policy base
  premium (the sum of all item base premiums); the processing fee is
  added at the very end

### Modifier thresholds

- customer with exactly 2 years with MHPCO → loyalty discount applies
- sword with exactly enchantment 5 → high-enchantment surcharge
  applies; if cursed, both surcharges apply
- sword with enchantment 4 → no high-enchantment surcharge; curse
  surcharge applies only if cursed
- dragon-material sword with exactly enchantment 8, damage 1000 G →
  payout 400 G (high-enchantment clause applies, then deductible)

### Deductible per damage event

- a dragon attack damages an insured sword (500 G) and an insured
  amulet (300 G); payout = 600 G (the 100 G deductible applies once
  per damaged item)

### Standard reimbursement (no special clauses)

- regular sword (steel, enchantment 3), damage 500 G → payout 400 G
  (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; no special clause applies)
- damage to a rune (insurance value 250 G), damage 200 G → payout
  100 G (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; runes have no
  enchantment level or material, so no special clause applies)

### Enchantment threshold vs. dragon material

- dragon-material sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
  (both clauses apply; the 50 % rule wins, then deductible: 500 − 100)
- dragon-material sword, enchantment 5, damage 800 G → payout 700 G
  (only the dragon-material clause applies: full reimbursement,
  then deductible: 800 − 100)
- steel sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
  (only the high-enchantment clause applies: 50 % first, then
  deductible: 500 − 100)

### Multiple items of the same type

> ❓ What if a customer brings two of the same item — one in hand,
> one in the backpack? Or just two swords because they fight with
> two?

- a policy covers two swords → insurance sum 2000 G (= 2×1000),
  cap 4000 G
- a dragon attack damages both swords; `damages` contains two
  `{itemType: "sword", ...}` entries → each entry is treated as a
  separate damage with its own deductible
- if the `damages` array contains more entries of a given type than
  the policy actually covers (e.g. two sword damages but only one
  sword insured) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code; the
  whole claim is rejected

### Cap exhaustion

- a policy covers a sword and an amulet → insurance sum 1600 G
  (= 1000 + 600, the sum of the items' insurance values), cap 3200 G
- a cursed sword (insurance value 1000 G, premium with modifiers
  165 G) → cap 2000 G (based on the unmodified insurance value;
  premium modifiers do not raise the cap)
- a policy covers a sword and 3 runes (a block) → insurance sum
  1750 G (= 1000 + 3×250); the block discount affects the premium
  only, not the insurance sum
- a sword is insured (insurance sum 1000 G, cap 2000 G); two successive
  claims of 1500 G each
- first claim → payout 1400 G, cap remaining 600 G
- second claim → payout 600 G, cap remaining 0 G (the desired 1400 G
  is reduced to the remaining cap)

### Rounding in the MHPCO's favor

- a premium calculation that yields 197.5 G → final premium 198 G
  (rounded up)
- a payout calculation that yields 350.5 G → final payout 350 G
  (rounded down)
- intermediate amounts during a calculation are kept as fractions;
  only the final premium or payout is rounded

### Edge cases

- empty item list → premium 5 G (only the processing fee)
- quote includes an item with an unknown type (e.g.
  `{type: "broomstick"}`) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
  and writes an error description to stderr; no `results` are written
  to stdout
- claim references a damage entry whose item is not part of the policy
  (e.g. an amulet damaged when only a sword is insured, or an item
  with an unknown type) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
  and writes an error description to stderr
- claim contains a damage entry with `amount: -200` → the CLI exits
  with a non-zero status code and writes an error description to
  stderr

## Integration examples

The following two examples combine multiple rules to clarify how
modifiers stack and how customer history affects the premium.

### Newcomer with a cursed sword

- customer: 0 years with MHPCO, no previous contract
- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 3)
- premium: 165 G
- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 10 G first insurance = 160 G + 5 G fee = 165 G)

### Long-standing customer's second contract

> ❓ Wait — does "first insurance" mean the customer's first ever
> contract, or the first time we see this particular item? A
> long-standing customer with a brand-new sword: does the first
> insurance surcharge still apply?

- customer: 3 years with MHPCO; this is the customer's second `quote`
  in the scenario
- item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7)
- premium: 160 G
- (100 G base + 50 G curse + 30 G high enchantment − 20 G loyalty
  + 10 G first insurance − 15 G follow-up contract = 155 G + 5 G fee
  = 160 G)
- The first insurance surcharge still applies to the new sword, even
  though the customer is on a follow-up contract — each item in a
  `quote` is treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer
  history.

## CLI input/output format

The implementation must be exposed as a command-line executable
`claim-office`. It reads a JSON document from stdin describing a
scenario and writes a JSON document with the corresponding results to
stdout.

A scenario is a JSON object with a `customer` object and a `steps`
array. The `customer` describes the single customer for the whole
scenario. Each step has an `op` field (`"quote"` or `"claim"`) and the
operation-specific input fields. Steps are processed sequentially;
later `claim` steps refer to a policy created by an earlier `quote`
step via its zero-based step index in a `policy` field.

Stdout receives a JSON object with a `results` array of the same length
and order as the input `steps`. Each result contains:

- For a `quote` step: `premium` (integer, total premium in G).
- For a `claim` step: `payout` (integer, payout in G) and
  `remainingCap` (integer, cap remaining on the policy after this
  claim).

### Schema example

Stdin:

```json
{
  "customer": {"yearsWithMHPCO": 5},
  "steps": [
    {
      "op": "quote",
      "items": [
        {"type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false}
      ]
    },
    {
      "op": "claim",
      "policy": 0,
      "incident": {
        "cause": "fire",
        "damages": [
          {"itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200}
        ]
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

Stdout shape:

```json
{"results": [{"premium": <integer>}, {"payout": <integer>, "remainingCap": <integer>}]}
```

## Expected Output Files

- Source files implementing the logic and the `claim-office` CLI
- Test files
- CLI entry point at `src/cli.ts` (reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout)

## Constraints

- Use TypeScript for the implementation
- The CLI must read JSON from stdin and write JSON to stdout
- The CLI entry point must be at `src/cli.ts`
- Internal data structures and module organization are free

skills.name: red
skills.content:
# TDD Red Phase

You are now in the **Red Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to activate ONE test and make it fail.

## Your Mission

1. Activate exactly ONE test from the test list
2. Make explicit predictions about how it will fail
3. Verify the test fails for the right reason
4. NO implementation during Red phase

## Context: Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Activate: "long-standing customer's second contract integration example -> 160"
Current passing tests: 19
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts

## Red Phase Rules

- **One test at a time**: Convert exactly ONE `it.todo()` to executable test
- **All other tests remain as `it.todo()`**: Never have more than one failing test
- **Two-stage failure**: First compilation error, then runtime/assertion error
- **Make predictions**: Explicitly state expected failures before running tests
- **No implementation**: Don't write code to make test pass yet

## Process

### Step 1: Activate One Test

Identify the next `it.todo()` and convert it to executable test code:

```typescript
// Convert from:
it.todo("should return 0 for empty input");

// To:
it("should return 0 for empty input", () => {
  expect(calculate("")).toBe(0);
});
```

Leave all other tests as `it.todo()`.

### Step 2: Predict Compilation Error

Before running the test, state your prediction:

```
🔴 Red Phase - Compilation Error Prediction:
- Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
- Expected: Compilation error
- Reason: Function `calculate` doesn't exist yet
- Error: "Cannot find name 'calculate'"
```

### Step 3: Run Test - Verify Compilation Error

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- ✅ Compilation error as predicted, OR
- ❌ Prediction wrong → STOP and explain discrepancy

### Step 4: Create Empty Function

Create minimal function stub (no logic):

```typescript
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return undefined as unknown as number;
};
```

### Step 5: Predict Runtime Error

Before running again, state your prediction:

```
🔴 Red Phase - Runtime Error Prediction:
- Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
- Expected: Runtime assertion error
- Expected value: 0
- Actual value: undefined
- Diff:
  Expected: 0
  Received: undefined
```

### Step 6: Run Test - Verify Runtime Error

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- ✅ Assertion error as predicted, OR
- ❌ Prediction wrong → STOP and explain discrepancy

### Step 7: Report Completion

You MUST output the full Step 7 block verbatim with `Correct` or `Incorrect`
chosen for each prediction. Do not abbreviate. Do not collapse the two
prediction lines into one.

```
🔴 Red Phase Complete:
**Test Activated**: "should return 0 for empty input"
**Compilation Prediction**: Cannot find name 'calculate' ✅ Correct
**Runtime Prediction**: Expected 0, received undefined ✅ Correct
**Result**: Test fails as expected with assertion error

Proceeding to Green phase.
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- ✅ Activate exactly ONE test at a time
- ✅ Make explicit predictions before running tests
- ✅ Verify test fails for the right reason
- ✅ Keep all other tests as `it.todo()`

### DON'T
- ❌ Activate multiple tests
- ❌ Skip making predictions
- ❌ Write implementation to make test pass
- ❌ Continue if prediction fails without explanation

## Prediction Failure Protocol

If your prediction was wrong:

```
❌ Prediction Failed:
- Predicted: [what you expected]
- Actual: [what happened]
- Discrepancy: [explanation]

Investigating the discrepancy before proceeding.
```

## Completion

After completing Red phase, proceed to Green phase:

```
🔴 Red Phase Complete. Proceeding to Green phase.
```

skills.name: green
skills.content:
# TDD Green Phase

You are now in the **Green Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to make the failing test pass with MINIMAL code.

## Your Mission

1. Implement the minimal code necessary to make the failing test pass
2. Use the simplest possible solution (hardcoded values are acceptable)
3. Do not add features for future tests
4. Verify all tests pass
5. No optimization, no refactoring during Green phase

## Context: Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Failing test: "follow-up contract (after first) gets 15% discount on policy base"
Current error: Second quote should be 100 (after 15% follow-up discount applied to second contract).
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts

## Green Phase Rules

- **Minimal code only**: Just enough to pass the current test
- **Smallest change**: Make the smallest possible change
- **No future features**: Don't implement what future tests might need
- **Hardcoded returns are allowed**
- **Tests must pass**: Verify all tests are green
- **No refactoring during Green phase**: Save improvements for Refactor phase

## Process

### Step 1: Analyze the Failing Test

Identify:
- What input does the test provide?
- What output does it expect?
- What is the simplest way to produce that output?

### Step 2: Write Minimal Implementation

Implement only what's needed to make the current test pass:

```typescript
// For first test "should return 0 for empty input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return 0;
};

// For second test "should return number for single input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  return parseInt(input);
};

// For third test "should add two numbers":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  const numbers = input.split(",");
  if (numbers.length === 1) return parseInt(numbers[0]);
  return parseInt(numbers[0]) + parseInt(numbers[1]);
};
```

### Step 3: Run Tests

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- Current test now passes ✅
- All previous tests still pass ✅

### Step 4: Verify No Over-Implementation

Check:
- ❓ Did I implement features for future tests? → Remove them
- ❓ Did I add logic not demanded by current test? → Remove it
- ❓ Did I optimize prematurely? → Simplify
- ❓ Did I refactor existing code? → Revert, save for Refactor phase

### Step 5: Report Completion

```
🟢 Green Phase Complete:
**Implementation**: [brief description of what was added]
**Result**: All tests now pass ([X] passing)

Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

## Minimal Implementation Strategies

### Hardcoded Returns

```typescript
// Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
return 0;
```

### Simple Conditionals

```typescript
// Test: "should return number for single input"
if (input === "") return 0;
return parseInt(input);
```

### Generalization (only when forced by a new test)

```typescript
// Test: "should add multiple numbers"
return input.split(",").reduce((sum, n) => sum + parseInt(n), 0);
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- ✅ Write minimal code to make test pass
- ✅ Use hardcoded values when appropriate
- ✅ Take small steps
- ✅ Verify all tests pass

### DON'T
- ❌ Implement beyond what tests demand
- ❌ Add features for future tests
- ❌ Optimize prematurely
- ❌ Refactor during Green phase

## Completion

After completing Green phase, proceed to Refactor phase:

```
🟢 Green Phase Complete. Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

skills.name: test-list
skills.content:
# TDD Test List Phase

You are now in the **Test List Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to create a comprehensive test list.

## Your Mission

Create a test list using `it.todo()` for BASE FUNCTIONALITY ONLY:
1. Identify the **core/base functionality** of the feature
2. Break it down into discrete, testable behaviors
3. Create test cases using `it.todo()` - NO executable tests yet
4. Order tests from simplest to most complex
5. Avoid advanced features or edge cases

## Context: Feature: MHPCO Claim Office Kata - policy management system with quote and claim operations.
Test file path: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file path: src/claim-office.ts
Requirements: See prompt.md for full specification. The system supports:
- quote: computes premium for items with base premiums (Sword 100, Amulet 60, Staff 80, Potion 40, Components 25 each, block of 3 alike = 60)
- claim: processes damage with 100G deductible per damage, payout capped at 2x insurance sum
- Modifiers: cursed +50%, high enchantment (≥5) +30%, loyalty (≥2 years) -20%, first insurance +10%, follow-up contract -15%, 5G processing fee
- Item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchant) apply to item base; policy-wide modifiers (loyalty, first, follow-up) apply to policy total
- Premium rounded UP, payout rounded DOWN
- Damage to enchantment ≥8: 50% reimbursement; dragon material: full reimbursement
- CLI at src/cli.ts reads JSON from stdin, writes results JSON to stdout

## Test List Rules

### What to Include (Base Functionality)
- **Empty/zero cases**: What happens with empty input?
- **Single element cases**: Simplest non-empty input
- **Two element cases**: Introduces interaction
- **Multiple element cases**: Generalizes the pattern
- **Basic validation**: Essential constraints only

### What to Exclude (Save for Later)
- Advanced features
- Edge cases
- Performance optimizations
- Exotic inputs
- Error handling beyond basics

## Process

### Step 1: Understand the Feature
- What is the core functionality?
- What are the **essential behaviors** (not nice-to-haves)?
- What is the **minimum viable feature**?

### Step 2: Identify Base Test Cases
List 3-6 test cases covering base functionality only.

### Step 3: Order Tests (Simple → Complex)
1. Simplest case (often empty/zero)
2. Single element
3. Two elements
4. Multiple elements
5. Basic validation

### Step 4: Write Test File
Create the test file with `it.todo()` entries:

```typescript
import { describe, it, expect } from "vitest";
import { functionName } from "./implementation.js";

describe("Feature Name", () => {
  it.todo("should [simplest case]");
  it.todo("should [next case]");
  it.todo("should [more complex case]");
  // ... ordered simple → complex
});
```

### Step 5: Provide Summary

After creating the test list, output:

```
📋 Test List Created:
**Feature**: [feature name]
**Test File**: [filename].spec.ts
**Base Functionality Tests**: [count]

**Test Cases** (ordered simple → complex):
1. ✅ [first test description]
2. ✅ [second test description]
3. ✅ [third test description]
...

**Advanced Features** (NOT included):
- [feature 1] - save for later
- [feature 2] - save for later

**Next Step**: Invoke `/red` to activate the first test.
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- ✅ Focus on **base functionality only**
- ✅ Order tests **simple → complex**
- ✅ Use `it.todo()` for all tests
- ✅ Write **clear, specific descriptions**
- ✅ Keep tests **independent**
- ✅ One behavior per test

### DON'T
- ❌ Include advanced features
- ❌ Write executable tests (use `it.todo()`)
- ❌ Think about implementation
- ❌ Include edge cases
- ❌ Order randomly

## Completion

After completing the test list, proceed to Red phase:

```
📋 Test List Phase Complete. Proceeding to Red phase with the first test.
```

# TDD Green Phase

You are now in the **Green Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to make the failing test pass with MINIMAL code.

## Your Mission

1. Implement the minimal code necessary to make the failing test pass
2. Use the simplest possible solution (hardcoded values are acceptable)
3. Do not add features for future tests
4. Verify all tests pass
5. No optimization, no refactoring during Green phase

## Context: Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Failing test: "unknown item type in quote throws an error"
Current error: AssertionError: expected [Function] to throw an error
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts

## Green Phase Rules

- **Minimal code only**: Just enough to pass the current test
- **Smallest change**: Make the smallest possible change
- **No future features**: Don't implement what future tests might need
- **Hardcoded returns are allowed**
- **Tests must pass**: Verify all tests are green
- **No refactoring during Green phase**: Save improvements for Refactor phase

## Process

### Step 1: Analyze the Failing Test

Identify:
- What input does the test provide?
- What output does it expect?
- What is the simplest way to produce that output?

### Step 2: Write Minimal Implementation

Implement only what's needed to make the current test pass:

```typescript
// For first test "should return 0 for empty input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return 0;
};

// For second test "should return number for single input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  return parseInt(input);
};

// For third test "should add two numbers":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  const numbers = input.split(",");
  if (numbers.length === 1) return parseInt(numbers[0]);
  return parseInt(numbers[0]) + parseInt(numbers[1]);
};
```

### Step 3: Run Tests

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- Current test now passes ✅
- All previous tests still pass ✅

### Step 4: Verify No Over-Implementation

Check:
- ❓ Did I implement features for future tests? → Remove them
- ❓ Did I add logic not demanded by current test? → Remove it
- ❓ Did I optimize prematurely? → Simplify
- ❓ Did I refactor existing code? → Revert, save for Refactor phase

### Step 5: Report Completion

```
🟢 Green Phase Complete:
**Implementation**: [brief description of what was added]
**Result**: All tests now pass ([X] passing)

Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

## Minimal Implementation Strategies

### Hardcoded Returns

```typescript
// Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
return 0;
```

### Simple Conditionals

```typescript
// Test: "should return number for single input"
if (input === "") return 0;
return parseInt(input);
```

### Generalization (only when forced by a new test)

```typescript
// Test: "should add multiple numbers"
return input.split(",").reduce((sum, n) => sum + parseInt(n), 0);
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- ✅ Write minimal code to make test pass
- ✅ Use hardcoded values when appropriate
- ✅ Take small steps
- ✅ Verify all tests pass

### DON'T
- ❌ Implement beyond what tests demand
- ❌ Add features for future tests
- ❌ Optimize prematurely
- ❌ Refactor during Green phase

## Completion

After completing Green phase, proceed to Refactor phase:

```
🟢 Green Phase Complete. Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

The refactoring is complete. The todo list isn't applicable here since this is a single focused refactoring task.

## Refactor Phase Summary

**Files modified**: `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-18_19-13-49_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.3-no-pep_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`

**Naming Evaluation (FIRST PRIORITY)**:
- All existing function names (`isComponent`, `isKnownType`, `validateItems`, `quote`, `policyBase`, `mainItemBase`, `mainItemSurcharge`, `componentsBase`, `loyaltyDiscount`, etc.) already clearly reveal intent. No renames needed.
- However, parameter names were improved: `items` → `mainItems` / `components` in functions that operate on already-filtered subsets, which **better reveals intent** at the call site.

**Refactoring Applied (Rule 2 - Reveals Intent, Rule 3 - No Duplication)**:

The original code passed unfiltered `items` to `mainItemsBase`, `componentsBase`, and `itemSurcharges`, with each function filtering internally. This had two problems:
1. **Hidden filtering**: Callers couldn't see what subset each function actually operated on
2. **Wasted work**: `itemSurcharges` mapped `mainItemSurcharge` over components (always returning 0)
3. **Conceptual duplication**: The "split items into mains vs. components" concept was scattered across helpers

**Change**: Hoisted the partitioning (`mainItems` / `components`) into `quote()`, the single place that owns the raw `step.items`. Helper functions now declare in their signatures exactly which subset they need.

**APP Mass Comparison**:

Before (relevant section in `quote` + helpers):
- `quote`: bindings(step,customer,isFollowUp,base,surcharges,firstInsurance,loyalty,followUp)=8, invocations(validateItems,policyBase,itemSurcharges,loyaltyDiscount,Math.ceil,*,*,+,+,-,-,+)=~24, conditionals(?:)=4
- `mainItemsBase`: invocations(sum,filter,map,!,isComponent)=10, bindings(items,item)=2
- `componentsBase`: invocations(countByType,filter,sum,spread,map)=10, bindings(items,counts)=2
- `itemSurcharges`: invocations(sum,map)=4, bindings(items)=1
- `policyBase`: invocations(mainItemsBase,componentsBase,+)=6, bindings(items)=1

After:
- `quote`: bindings(step,customer,isFollowUp,mainItems,components,base,surcharges,firstInsurance,loyalty,followUp)=10, invocations(validateItems,filter,!,isComponent,filter,isComponent,policyBase,itemSurcharges,loyaltyDiscount,Math.ceil,...)=~30, conditionals(?:)=4
- `mainItemsBase`: invocations(sum,map)=4, bindings(mainItems)=1 — **mass reduced** (-6 from removed filter+!+isComponent chain)
- `componentsBase`: invocations(countByType,sum,spread,map)=8, bindings(components,counts)=2 — **mass reduced** (-2 from removed filter+isComponent)
- `itemSurcharges`: unchanged structure (mass: ~5)
- `policyBase`: invocations(mainItemsBase,componentsBase,+)=6, bindings(mainItems,components)=2 — same

**Net mass change**: Slight increase in `quote` (~+10 from explicit partitioning) offset by reductions in 3 helpers (~-8 total). Mass is approximately neutral, but **clarity (Rule 2) is significantly improved** — Rule 2 trumps APP.

**Benefits**:
- Helper function signatures now express their preconditions (e.g., `mainItemsBase` clearly takes already-filtered main items)
- Partitioning happens once at the boundary, where it conceptually belongs
- No more wasted iterations of `mainItemSurcharge` over components
- Removes scattered `filter(isComponent)` / `filter(!isComponent)` calls (Rule 3)

**Tests**: All 21 passing, 15 todo remain. No regressions.

# TDD Red Phase

You are now in the **Red Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to activate ONE test and make it fail.

## Your Mission

1. Activate exactly ONE test from the test list
2. Make explicit predictions about how it will fail
3. Verify the test fails for the right reason
4. NO implementation during Red phase

## Context: Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Activate: "regular sword damage 500 returns payout 400 (500 - 100 deductible)"
Current passing tests: 21
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts

## Red Phase Rules

- **One test at a time**: Convert exactly ONE `it.todo()` to executable test
- **All other tests remain as `it.todo()`**: Never have more than one failing test
- **Two-stage failure**: First compilation error, then runtime/assertion error
- **Make predictions**: Explicitly state expected failures before running tests
- **No implementation**: Don't write code to make test pass yet

## Process

### Step 1: Activate One Test

Identify the next `it.todo()` and convert it to executable test code:

```typescript
// Convert from:
it.todo("should return 0 for empty input");

// To:
it("should return 0 for empty input", () => {
  expect(calculate("")).toBe(0);
});
```

Leave all other tests as `it.todo()`.

### Step 2: Predict Compilation Error

Before running the test, state your prediction:

```
🔴 Red Phase - Compilation Error Prediction:
- Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
- Expected: Compilation error
- Reason: Function `calculate` doesn't exist yet
- Error: "Cannot find name 'calculate'"
```

### Step 3: Run Test - Verify Compilation Error

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- ✅ Compilation error as predicted, OR
- ❌ Prediction wrong → STOP and explain discrepancy

### Step 4: Create Empty Function

Create minimal function stub (no logic):

```typescript
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return undefined as unknown as number;
};
```

### Step 5: Predict Runtime Error

Before running again, state your prediction:

```
🔴 Red Phase - Runtime Error Prediction:
- Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
- Expected: Runtime assertion error
- Expected value: 0
- Actual value: undefined
- Diff:
  Expected: 0
  Received: undefined
```

### Step 6: Run Test - Verify Runtime Error

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- ✅ Assertion error as predicted, OR
- ❌ Prediction wrong → STOP and explain discrepancy

### Step 7: Report Completion

You MUST output the full Step 7 block verbatim with `Correct` or `Incorrect`
chosen for each prediction. Do not abbreviate. Do not collapse the two
prediction lines into one.

```
🔴 Red Phase Complete:
**Test Activated**: "should return 0 for empty input"
**Compilation Prediction**: Cannot find name 'calculate' ✅ Correct
**Runtime Prediction**: Expected 0, received undefined ✅ Correct
**Result**: Test fails as expected with assertion error

Proceeding to Green phase.
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- ✅ Activate exactly ONE test at a time
- ✅ Make explicit predictions before running tests
- ✅ Verify test fails for the right reason
- ✅ Keep all other tests as `it.todo()`

### DON'T
- ❌ Activate multiple tests
- ❌ Skip making predictions
- ❌ Write implementation to make test pass
- ❌ Continue if prediction fails without explanation

## Prediction Failure Protocol

If your prediction was wrong:

```
❌ Prediction Failed:
- Predicted: [what you expected]
- Actual: [what happened]
- Discrepancy: [explanation]

Investigating the discrepancy before proceeding.
```

## Completion

After completing Red phase, proceed to Green phase:

```
🔴 Red Phase Complete. Proceeding to Green phase.
```

# TDD Green Phase

You are now in the **Green Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to make the failing test pass with MINIMAL code.

## Your Mission

1. Implement the minimal code necessary to make the failing test pass
2. Use the simplest possible solution (hardcoded values are acceptable)
3. Do not add features for future tests
4. Verify all tests pass
5. No optimization, no refactoring during Green phase

## Context: Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Failing test: "regular sword damage 500 returns payout 400 (500 - 100 deductible)"
Current error: TypeError: items is not iterable (runScenario treats claim step as quote)
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts

Need to: dispatch on step.op (quote vs claim), implement claim that produces { payout, remainingCap }. Sword insurance value 1000, cap = 2x = 2000. Payout = damage - 100 deductible = 400. Remaining cap = 2000 - 400 = 1600.

## Green Phase Rules

- **Minimal code only**: Just enough to pass the current test
- **Smallest change**: Make the smallest possible change
- **No future features**: Don't implement what future tests might need
- **Hardcoded returns are allowed**
- **Tests must pass**: Verify all tests are green
- **No refactoring during Green phase**: Save improvements for Refactor phase

## Process

### Step 1: Analyze the Failing Test

Identify:
- What input does the test provide?
- What output does it expect?
- What is the simplest way to produce that output?

### Step 2: Write Minimal Implementation

Implement only what's needed to make the current test pass:

```typescript
// For first test "should return 0 for empty input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return 0;
};

// For second test "should return number for single input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  return parseInt(input);
};

// For third test "should add two numbers":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  const numbers = input.split(",");
  if (numbers.length === 1) return parseInt(numbers[0]);
  return parseInt(numbers[0]) + parseInt(numbers[1]);
};
```

### Step 3: Run Tests

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- Current test now passes ✅
- All previous tests still pass ✅

### Step 4: Verify No Over-Implementation

Check:
- ❓ Did I implement features for future tests? → Remove them
- ❓ Did I add logic not demanded by current test? → Remove it
- ❓ Did I optimize prematurely? → Simplify
- ❓ Did I refactor existing code? → Revert, save for Refactor phase

### Step 5: Report Completion

```
🟢 Green Phase Complete:
**Implementation**: [brief description of what was added]
**Result**: All tests now pass ([X] passing)

Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

## Minimal Implementation Strategies

### Hardcoded Returns

```typescript
// Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
return 0;
```

### Simple Conditionals

```typescript
// Test: "should return number for single input"
if (input === "") return 0;
return parseInt(input);
```

### Generalization (only when forced by a new test)

```typescript
// Test: "should add multiple numbers"
return input.split(",").reduce((sum, n) => sum + parseInt(n), 0);
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- ✅ Write minimal code to make test pass
- ✅ Use hardcoded values when appropriate
- ✅ Take small steps
- ✅ Verify all tests pass

### DON'T
- ❌ Implement beyond what tests demand
- ❌ Add features for future tests
- ❌ Optimize prematurely
- ❌ Refactor during Green phase

## Completion

After completing Green phase, proceed to Refactor phase:

```
🟢 Green Phase Complete. Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

All 22 tests still passing. Let me also evaluate the `claim()` function — the variable `totalPayout` calculation uses `damages.map(d => d.amount - DEDUCTIBLE)`. Naming-wise it might be better as `payout` since it's already the total.

Looking at the claim function:
```typescript
const claim = (step: ClaimStep, policy: Policy): ClaimResult => {
  const totalPayout = sum(step.incident.damages.map((d) => d.amount - DEDUCTIBLE));
  return { payout: totalPayout, remainingCap: policy.cap - totalPayout };
};
```

The name `totalPayout` is fine — it accurately describes summing payouts across damages, and the binding helps avoid duplicating the expression. This is appropriate.

**Refactoring Summary:**

- All function and variable names already clearly reveal intent
- Introduced explicit `isFollowUp` local binding to make the boolean condition self-documenting at the call site (previously the inline `quoteCount > 0` required mental translation)

content.text:
# TDD Red Phase

You are now in the **Red Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to activate ONE test and make it fail.

## Your Mission

1. Activate exactly ONE test from the test list
2. Make explicit predictions about how it will fail
3. Verify the test fails for the right reason
4. NO implementation during Red phase

## Context: Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Activate: "enchantment 8+ item damage is reimbursed at 50% then deductible"
Current passing tests: 23
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts

## Red Phase Rules

- **One test at a time**: Convert exactly ONE `it.todo()` to executable test
- **All other tests remain as `it.todo()`**: Never have more than one failing test
- **Two-stage failure**: First compilation error, then runtime/assertion error
- **Make predictions**: Explicitly state expected failures before running tests
- **No implementation**: Don't write code to make test pass yet

## Process

### Step 1: Activate One Test

Identify the next `it.todo()` and convert it to executable test code:

```typescript
// Convert from:
it.todo("should return 0 for empty input");

// To:
it("should return 0 for empty input", () => {
  expect(calculate("")).toBe(0);
});
```

Leave all other tests as `it.todo()`.

### Step 2: Predict Compilation Error

Before running the test, state your prediction:

```
🔴 Red Phase - Compilation Error Prediction:
- Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
- Expected: Compilation error
- Reason: Function `calculate` doesn't exist yet
- Error: "Cannot find name 'calculate'"
```

### Step 3: Run Test - Verify Compilation Error

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- ✅ Compilation error as predicted, OR
- ❌ Prediction wrong → STOP and explain discrepancy

### Step 4: Create Empty Function

Create minimal function stub (no logic):

```typescript
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return undefined as unknown as number;
};
```

### Step 5: Predict Runtime Error

Before running again, state your prediction:

```
🔴 Red Phase - Runtime Error Prediction:
- Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
- Expected: Runtime assertion error
- Expected value: 0
- Actual value: undefined
- Diff:
  Expected: 0
  Received: undefined
```

### Step 6: Run Test - Verify Runtime Error

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- ✅ Assertion error as predicted, OR
- ❌ Prediction wrong → STOP and explain discrepancy

### Step 7: Report Completion

You MUST output the full Step 7 block verbatim with `Correct` or `Incorrect`
chosen for each prediction. Do not abbreviate. Do not collapse the two
prediction lines into one.

```
🔴 Red Phase Complete:
**Test Activated**: "should return 0 for empty input"
**Compilation Prediction**: Cannot find name 'calculate' ✅ Correct
**Runtime Prediction**: Expected 0, received undefined ✅ Correct
**Result**: Test fails as expected with assertion error

Proceeding to Green phase.
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- ✅ Activate exactly ONE test at a time
- ✅ Make explicit predictions before running tests
- ✅ Verify test fails for the right reason
- ✅ Keep all other tests as `it.todo()`

### DON'T
- ❌ Activate multiple tests
- ❌ Skip making predictions
- ❌ Write implementation to make test pass
- ❌ Continue if prediction fails without explanation

## Prediction Failure Protocol

If your prediction was wrong:

```
❌ Prediction Failed:
- Predicted: [what you expected]
- Actual: [what happened]
- Discrepancy: [explanation]

Investigating the discrepancy before proceeding.
```

## Completion

After completing Red phase, proceed to Green phase:

```
🔴 Red Phase Complete. Proceeding to Green phase.
```

content.text:
# TDD Green Phase

You are now in the **Green Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to make the failing test pass with MINIMAL code.

## Your Mission

1. Implement the minimal code necessary to make the failing test pass
2. Use the simplest possible solution (hardcoded values are acceptable)
3. Do not add features for future tests
4. Verify all tests pass
5. No optimization, no refactoring during Green phase

## Context: Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Failing test: "enchantment 8+ item damage is reimbursed at 50% then deductible"
Current error: Expected payout 400, received 900. claim() needs to look up the damaged item in the policy and apply 50% reimbursement when enchantment >= 8 before deductible.
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts

## Green Phase Rules

- **Minimal code only**: Just enough to pass the current test
- **Smallest change**: Make the smallest possible change
- **No future features**: Don't implement what future tests might need
- **Hardcoded returns are allowed**
- **Tests must pass**: Verify all tests are green
- **No refactoring during Green phase**: Save improvements for Refactor phase

## Process

### Step 1: Analyze the Failing Test

Identify:
- What input does the test provide?
- What output does it expect?
- What is the simplest way to produce that output?

### Step 2: Write Minimal Implementation

Implement only what's needed to make the current test pass:

```typescript
// For first test "should return 0 for empty input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return 0;
};

// For second test "should return number for single input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  return parseInt(input);
};

// For third test "should add two numbers":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  const numbers = input.split(",");
  if (numbers.length === 1) return parseInt(numbers[0]);
  return parseInt(numbers[0]) + parseInt(numbers[1]);
};
```

### Step 3: Run Tests

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- Current test now passes ✅
- All previous tests still pass ✅

### Step 4: Verify No Over-Implementation

Check:
- ❓ Did I implement features for future tests? → Remove them
- ❓ Did I add logic not demanded by current test? → Remove it
- ❓ Did I optimize prematurely? → Simplify
- ❓ Did I refactor existing code? → Revert, save for Refactor phase

### Step 5: Report Completion

```
🟢 Green Phase Complete:
**Implementation**: [brief description of what was added]
**Result**: All tests now pass ([X] passing)

Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

## Minimal Implementation Strategies

### Hardcoded Returns

```typescript
// Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
return 0;
```

### Simple Conditionals

```typescript
// Test: "should return number for single input"
if (input === "") return 0;
return parseInt(input);
```

### Generalization (only when forced by a new test)

```typescript
// Test: "should add multiple numbers"
return input.split(",").reduce((sum, n) => sum + parseInt(n), 0);
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- ✅ Write minimal code to make test pass
- ✅ Use hardcoded values when appropriate
- ✅ Take small steps
- ✅ Verify all tests pass

### DON'T
- ❌ Implement beyond what tests demand
- ❌ Add features for future tests
- ❌ Optimize prematurely
- ❌ Refactor during Green phase

## Completion

After completing Green phase, proceed to Refactor phase:

```
🟢 Green Phase Complete. Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

### Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

**Refactored `damagePayout` + new `reimbursementRate` helper**:

```typescript
const reimbursementRate = (item: Item | undefined): number =>
  item && (item.enchantment ?? 0) >= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_CLAIM_THRESHOLD
    ? HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_REIMBURSEMENT_RATE
    : 1;

const damagePayout = (damage: Damage, policy: Policy): number => {
  const item = policy.items.find((i) => i.type === damage.itemType);
  return damage.amount * reimbursementRate(item) - DEDUCTIBLE;
};
```

## Red Phase Rules

- **All other tests remain as `it.todo()`**: Never have more than one failing test

- **No implementation**: Don't write code to make test pass yet

## Process

### Step 1: Activate One Test

Identify the next `it.todo()` and convert it to executable test code:

```typescript
// Convert from:
it.todo("should return 0 for empty input");

// To:
it("should return 0 for empty input", () => {
  expect(calculate("")).toBe(0);
});
```

Leave all other tests as `it.todo()`.

### Step 2: Predict Compilation Error

Before running the test, state your prediction:

```
🔴 Red Phase - Compilation Error Prediction:
- Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
- Expected: Compilation error
- Reason: Function `calculate` doesn't exist yet
- Error: "Cannot find name 'calculate'"
```

### Step 3: Run Test - Verify Compilation Error

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- ✅ Compilation error as predicted, OR
- ❌ Prediction wrong → STOP and explain discrepancy

### Step 4: Create Empty Function

Create minimal function stub (no logic):

```typescript
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return undefined as unknown as number;
};
```

### Step 5: Predict Runtime Error

Before running again, state your prediction:

```
🔴 Red Phase - Runtime Error Prediction:
- Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
- Expected: Runtime assertion error
- Expected value: 0
- Actual value: undefined
- Diff:

  Received: undefined
```

### Step 6: Run Test - Verify Runtime Error

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- ✅ Assertion error as predicted, OR
- ❌ Prediction wrong → STOP and explain discrepancy

You MUST output the full Step 7 block verbatim with `Correct` or `Incorrect`
chosen for each prediction. Do not abbreviate. Do not collapse the two
prediction lines into one.

```

**Compilation Prediction**: Cannot find name 'calculate' ✅ Correct
**Runtime Prediction**: Expected 0, received undefined ✅ Correct
**Result**: Test fails as expected with assertion error

Proceeding to Green phase.
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- ✅ Activate exactly ONE test at a time
- ✅ Make explicit predictions before running tests
- ✅ Verify test fails for the right reason
- ✅ Keep all other tests as `it.todo()`

### DON'T
- ❌ Activate multiple tests
- ❌ Skip making predictions
- ❌ Write implementation to make test pass
- ❌ Continue if prediction fails without explanation

## Prediction Failure Protocol

If your prediction was wrong:

```
❌ Prediction Failed:
- Predicted: [what you expected]
- Actual: [what happened]
- Discrepancy: [explanation]

Investigating the discrepancy before proceeding.
```

content.text:
🔴 Red Phase: Test removed as duplicate. Moving to next.

But wait — I should reconsider. The spec actually has subtle behavior with dragon material that differs from "no clause" in a different scenario. Specifically, what about steel sword ench 8 vs dragon ench 8? Per spec example: "dragon-material sword with exactly enchantment 8, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G (high-enchantment clause applies, then deductible)".

So for dragon+ench8, payout=400 because 50% clause wins. My current implementation gets that right since ench>=8 triggers 50% regardless of material.

But what about a case where dragon material differs from default? Currently dragon material is functionally identical to default. The kata says dragon material is "fully reimbursed" — but full reimbursement IS the default. So dragon material is only meaningful as a way to OVERRIDE the 50% clause... but the spec also says 50% wins when both apply.

Actually, re-reading: "dragon-material sword, enchantment 5, damage 800 G → payout 700 G (only the dragon-material clause applies: full reimbursement, then deductible: 800 − 100)". And steel sword without high enchantment also gives full reimbursement. So dragon material is functionally a no-op when combined with current rules.

This makes the dragon material clause undertested — but that's a spec quality issue, not an implementation issue. My implementation is correct for all stated examples. Moving on.

# TDD Green Phase

You are now in the **Green Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to make the failing test pass with MINIMAL code.

## Your Mission

1. Implement the minimal code necessary to make the failing test pass
2. Use the simplest possible solution (hardcoded values are acceptable)
3. Do not add features for future tests

5. No optimization, no refactoring during Green phase

Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts

## Green Phase Rules

- **Minimal code only**: Just enough to pass the current test
- **Smallest change**: Make the smallest possible change
- **No future features**: Don't implement what future tests might need
- **Hardcoded returns are allowed**
- **Tests must pass**: Verify all tests are green
- **No refactoring during Green phase**: Save improvements for Refactor phase

## Process

### Step 1: Analyze the Failing Test

Identify:
- What input does the test provide?
- What output does it expect?
- What is the simplest way to produce that output?

### Step 2: Write Minimal Implementation

Implement only what's needed to make the current test pass:

```typescript
// For first test "should return 0 for empty input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return 0;
};

// For second test "should return number for single input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  return parseInt(input);
};

// For third test "should add two numbers":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  const numbers = input.split(",");
  if (numbers.length === 1) return parseInt(numbers[0]);
  return parseInt(numbers[0]) + parseInt(numbers[1]);
};
```

### Step 3: Run Tests

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- Current test now passes ✅
- All previous tests still pass ✅

### Step 4: Verify No Over-Implementation

Check:
- ❓ Did I implement features for future tests? → Remove them
- ❓ Did I add logic not demanded by current test? → Remove it
- ❓ Did I optimize prematurely? → Simplify
- ❓ Did I refactor existing code? → Revert, save for Refactor phase

### Step 5: Report Completion

```
🟢 Green Phase Complete:
**Implementation**: [brief description of what was added]
**Result**: All tests now pass ([X] passing)

Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

### Simple Conditionals

```typescript
// Test: "should return number for single input"
if (input === "") return 0;
return parseInt(input);
```

### Generalization (only when forced by a new test)

```typescript
// Test: "should add multiple numbers"
return input.split(",").reduce((sum, n) => sum + parseInt(n), 0);
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- ✅ Write minimal code to make test pass
- ✅ Use hardcoded values when appropriate
- ✅ Take small steps
- ✅ Verify all tests pass

### DON'T
- ❌ Implement beyond what tests demand
- ❌ Add features for future tests
- ❌ Optimize prematurely
- ❌ Refactor during Green phase

## Completion

After completing Green phase, proceed to Refactor phase:

```
🟢 Green Phase Complete. Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

content.text:
All 28 tests still pass.

**After Refactoring**:

```typescript
const totalDamagePayout = (damages: Damage[], policy: Policy): number =>
  sum(damages.map((d) => damagePayout(d, policy)));

const claim = (step: ClaimStep, policy: Policy): ClaimResult => {
  const payout = Math.min(totalDamagePayout(step.incident.damages, policy), policy.cap);
  return { payout, remainingCap: policy.cap - payout };
};
```

**After Mass**:
- `totalDamagePayout`: bindings 3 (damages, policy, d) + invocations 3 (sum, map, damagePayout) + loop 1 = 3 + 6 + 5 = 14
- `claim`: bindings 4 (step, policy, payout, payout-refs ×2) ≈ 5 + invocations 3 (Math.min, totalDamagePayout, -) = 6 = 11
- Total combined: ~25

Mass slightly increased due to the new function (extra parameter passing). However, the refactoring improves **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent)**:

1. **Separation of concerns**: `totalDamagePayout` clearly names the concept of "total raw payout from all damages" — now reusable.
2. **`claim()` is now more focused**: It only handles the cap logic and result construction.
3. **Removed intermediate variable**: `rawPayout` is no longer needed; the meaning is conveyed by the function name.
4. **Future-proof**: Upcoming tests about "successive claims reduce the remaining cap" will benefit from the cleaner separation.

**Refactoring Summary**:

- **Naming Evaluation**: All function names clearly reveal intent. The new `totalDamagePayout` helper is well-named.
- **Rule 1 (Tests Pass)**: All 28 tests still pass.
- **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent)**: Improved — extracted `totalDamagePayout` makes the two-step nature of claim payout (compute total, then cap) explicit through function names rather than intermediate variables.
- **Rule 3 (No Duplication)**: No new duplication; the helper is now available for reuse in future tests.
- **Rule 4 (Fewest Elements)**: Mass slightly increased (~22 → ~25) due to function extraction, but Rule 2 trumps APP per Simple Design priorities.

You are now in the **Red Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to activate ONE test and make it fail.

## Your Mission

1. Activate exactly ONE test from the test list
2. Make explicit predictions about how it will fail
3. Verify the test fails for the right reason
4. NO implementation during Red phase

Current passing tests: 28
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts

## Red Phase Rules

- **One test at a time**: Convert exactly ONE `it.todo()` to executable test
- **All other tests remain as `it.todo()`**: Never have more than one failing test
- **Two-stage failure**: First compilation error, then runtime/assertion error
- **Make predictions**: Explicitly state expected failures before running tests
- **No implementation**: Don't write code to make test pass yet

## Process

### Step 1: Activate One Test

Identify the next `it.todo()` and convert it to executable test code:

```typescript
// Convert from:
it.todo("should return 0 for empty input");

// To:
it("should return 0 for empty input", () => {
  expect(calculate("")).toBe(0);
});
```

Leave all other tests as `it.todo()`.

### Step 2: Predict Compilation Error

Before running the test, state your prediction:

```
🔴 Red Phase - Compilation Error Prediction:
- Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
- Expected: Compilation error
- Reason: Function `calculate` doesn't exist yet
- Error: "Cannot find name 'calculate'"
```

### Step 3: Run Test - Verify Compilation Error

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- ✅ Compilation error as predicted, OR
- ❌ Prediction wrong → STOP and explain discrepancy

### Step 4: Create Empty Function

Create minimal function stub (no logic):

```typescript
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return undefined as unknown as number;
};
```

### Step 5: Predict Runtime Error

Before running again, state your prediction:

```
🔴 Red Phase - Runtime Error Prediction:
- Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
- Expected: Runtime assertion error
- Expected value: 0
- Actual value: undefined
- Diff:
  Expected: 0
  Received: undefined
```

### Step 6: Run Test - Verify Runtime Error

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- ✅ Assertion error as predicted, OR
- ❌ Prediction wrong → STOP and explain discrepancy

### Step 7: Report Completion

You MUST output the full Step 7 block verbatim with `Correct` or `Incorrect`
chosen for each prediction. Do not abbreviate. Do not collapse the two
prediction lines into one.

```

**Compilation Prediction**: Cannot find name 'calculate' ✅ Correct
**Runtime Prediction**: Expected 0, received undefined ✅ Correct
**Result**: Test fails as expected with assertion error

Proceeding to Green phase.
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- ✅ Activate exactly ONE test at a time
- ✅ Make explicit predictions before running tests
- ✅ Verify test fails for the right reason
- ✅ Keep all other tests as `it.todo()`

### DON'T
- ❌ Activate multiple tests
- ❌ Skip making predictions
- ❌ Write implementation to make test pass
- ❌ Continue if prediction fails without explanation

## Prediction Failure Protocol

If your prediction was wrong:

```
❌ Prediction Failed:
- Predicted: [what you expected]
- Actual: [what happened]
- Discrepancy: [explanation]

Investigating the discrepancy before proceeding.
```

## Completion

After completing Red phase, proceed to Green phase:

```
🔴 Red Phase Complete. Proceeding to Green phase.
```

All 30 tests still pass. Now let me look further. The `dragon` material is referenced in tests but I don't see special handling — let me check `reimbursementRate`:

Looking at the test "dragon-material item damage is fully reimbursed then deductible" with `material: "dragon"`, `enchantment: 5`, damage 800 → payout 700. Since enchantment 5 < 8, `reimbursementRate` returns 1, so 800 × 1 - 100 = 700. ✓

And "dragon + enchantment 9: 50% rule wins" — enchantment 9 ≥ 8, so 0.5 rate. 1000 × 0.5 - 100 = 400. ✓

So dragon material isn't actually special — the test name is misleading but the behavior works because non-dragon non-enchanted items go through the same path. Good — no refactoring needed there.

**Naming evaluation for `claim()`:** After adding the `rawPayout` variable, the function reveals intent better:
- Compute raw payout from damages
- Clamp to remaining cap
- Decrement remaining cap
- Return both

Let me also check the `runScenario` switch logic. Could `policies[step.policy]` fail if the index is wrong? That's an error case (not in our test list), so not a concern.

**APP Mass comparison for `claim()`:**

Before:
- Bindings: step, policy, payout = 3
- Member access (count as bindings): step.incident.damages, policy.remainingCap (×3) = 4 → 7
- Invocations: Math.min, totalDamagePayout = 2 → 4
- Assignments: 1 → 6
- Constants: 0

Total ≈ 17

After:
- Bindings: step, policy, rawPayout, payout = 4
- Member access (count as bindings): step.incident.damages, policy.remainingCap (×3) = 4 → 8
- Invocations: Math.min, totalDamagePayout = 2 → 4
- Assignments: 1 → 6
- Constants: 0

Total ≈ 18

Mass increased by 1 (added explaining variable), but **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) trumps APP**. The split makes it clear that there are two distinct operations: computing the raw amount and clamping to the remaining cap.

- `claim()`, `quote()`, `runScenario()`, `damagePayout()`, `totalDamagePayout()`, `reimbursementRate()`, `policyBase()`, `mainItemSurcharge()`, `loyaltyDiscount()`, `insuranceSum()`, `componentTypeBase()`, `countByType()`, `validateItems()`, `isComponent()`, `isKnownType()` — all clearly reveal intent. Domain language is consistent. No renames needed.

**Refactoring applied:**
- Extracted explaining variable `rawPayout` in `claim()` to separate "compute total damage payout" from "clamp to remaining cap". This makes the cap-clamping logic explicit and easier to read.
- Rule applied: Rule 2 (Reveals Intent)
- Mass change: 17 → 18 (Δ +1) — accepted because Rule 2 trumps APP.

**Rules 3 and 4 evaluation:**
- Rule 3 (No Duplication): No new duplication. The helper functions already extract common logic well (`sum`, `mainItemBase`, `componentTypeBase`, `loyaltyDiscount`, `insuranceSum`, `reimbursementRate`).
- Rule 4 (Fewest Elements): All helpers serve a clear purpose. No unnecessary abstractions to remove.

# TDD Red Phase

You are now in the **Red Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to activate ONE test and make it fail.

## Your Mission

1. Activate exactly ONE test from the test list
2. Make explicit predictions about how it will fail
3. Verify the test fails for the right reason
4. NO implementation during Red phase

## Context: Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Activate: "policy with two swords: insurance sum 2000, cap 4000"
Current passing tests: 30
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts

## Red Phase Rules

- **One test at a time**: Convert exactly ONE `it.todo()` to executable test
- **All other tests remain as `it.todo()`**: Never have more than one failing test
- **Two-stage failure**: First compilation error, then runtime/assertion error
- **Make predictions**: Explicitly state expected failures before running tests
- **No implementation**: Don't write code to make test pass yet

## Process

### Step 1: Activate One Test

Identify the next `it.todo()` and convert it to executable test code:

```typescript
// Convert from:
it.todo("should return 0 for empty input");

// To:
it("should return 0 for empty input", () => {
  expect(calculate("")).toBe(0);
});
```

Leave all other tests as `it.todo()`.

### Step 2: Predict Compilation Error

Before running the test, state your prediction:

```
🔴 Red Phase - Compilation Error Prediction:
- Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
- Expected: Compilation error
- Reason: Function `calculate` doesn't exist yet
- Error: "Cannot find name 'calculate'"
```

### Step 3: Run Test - Verify Compilation Error

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- ✅ Compilation error as predicted, OR
- ❌ Prediction wrong → STOP and explain discrepancy

### Step 4: Create Empty Function

Create minimal function stub (no logic):

```typescript
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return undefined as unknown as number;
};
```

### Step 5: Predict Runtime Error

Before running again, state your prediction:

```
🔴 Red Phase - Runtime Error Prediction:
- Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
- Expected: Runtime assertion error
- Expected value: 0
- Actual value: undefined
- Diff:
  Expected: 0
  Received: undefined
```

### Step 6: Run Test - Verify Runtime Error

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- ✅ Assertion error as predicted, OR
- ❌ Prediction wrong → STOP and explain discrepancy

### Step 7: Report Completion

You MUST output the full Step 7 block verbatim with `Correct` or `Incorrect`
chosen for each prediction. Do not abbreviate. Do not collapse the two
prediction lines into one.

```
🔴 Red Phase Complete:
**Test Activated**: "should return 0 for empty input"
**Compilation Prediction**: Cannot find name 'calculate' ✅ Correct
**Runtime Prediction**: Expected 0, received undefined ✅ Correct
**Result**: Test fails as expected with assertion error

Proceeding to Green phase.
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- ✅ Activate exactly ONE test at a time
- ✅ Make explicit predictions before running tests
- ✅ Verify test fails for the right reason
- ✅ Keep all other tests as `it.todo()`

### DON'T
- ❌ Activate multiple tests
- ❌ Skip making predictions
- ❌ Write implementation to make test pass
- ❌ Continue if prediction fails without explanation

## Prediction Failure Protocol

If your prediction was wrong:

```
❌ Prediction Failed:
- Predicted: [what you expected]
- Actual: [what happened]
- Discrepancy: [explanation]

Investigating the discrepancy before proceeding.
```

## Completion

After completing Red phase, proceed to Green phase:

```
🔴 Red Phase Complete. Proceeding to Green phase.
```

# TDD Green Phase

You are now in the **Green Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to make the failing test pass with MINIMAL code.

## Your Mission

1. Implement the minimal code necessary to make the failing test pass
2. Use the simplest possible solution (hardcoded values are acceptable)
3. Do not add features for future tests
4. Verify all tests pass
5. No optimization, no refactoring during Green phase

## Context: Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Failing test: "damage entries exceeding insured count of that type rejects claim"
Current error: AssertionError - expected to throw. claim() needs to validate that damage count per type does not exceed the insured count per type.
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts

## Green Phase Rules

- **Minimal code only**: Just enough to pass the current test
- **Smallest change**: Make the smallest possible change
- **No future features**: Don't implement what future tests might need
- **Hardcoded returns are allowed**
- **Tests must pass**: Verify all tests are green
- **No refactoring during Green phase**: Save improvements for Refactor phase

## Process

### Step 1: Analyze the Failing Test

Identify:
- What input does the test provide?
- What output does it expect?
- What is the simplest way to produce that output?

### Step 2: Write Minimal Implementation

Implement only what's needed to make the current test pass:

```typescript
// For first test "should return 0 for empty input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return 0;
};

// For second test "should return number for single input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  return parseInt(input);
};

// For third test "should add two numbers":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  const numbers = input.split(",");
  if (numbers.length === 1) return parseInt(numbers[0]);
  return parseInt(numbers[0]) + parseInt(numbers[1]);
};
```

### Step 3: Run Tests

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- Current test now passes ✅
- All previous tests still pass ✅

### Step 4: Verify No Over-Implementation

Check:
- ❓ Did I implement features for future tests? → Remove them
- ❓ Did I add logic not demanded by current test? → Remove it
- ❓ Did I optimize prematurely? → Simplify
- ❓ Did I refactor existing code? → Revert, save for Refactor phase

### Step 5: Report Completion

```
🟢 Green Phase Complete:
**Implementation**: [brief description of what was added]
**Result**: All tests now pass ([X] passing)

Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

## Minimal Implementation Strategies

### Hardcoded Returns

```typescript
// Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
return 0;
```

### Simple Conditionals

```typescript
// Test: "should return number for single input"
if (input === "") return 0;
return parseInt(input);
```

### Generalization (only when forced by a new test)

```typescript
// Test: "should add multiple numbers"
return input.split(",").reduce((sum, n) => sum + parseInt(n), 0);
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- ✅ Write minimal code to make test pass
- ✅ Use hardcoded values when appropriate
- ✅ Take small steps
- ✅ Verify all tests pass

### DON'T
- ❌ Implement beyond what tests demand
- ❌ Add features for future tests
- ❌ Optimize prematurely
- ❌ Refactor during Green phase

## Completion

After completing Green phase, proceed to Refactor phase:

```
🟢 Green Phase Complete. Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

# TDD Red Phase

You are now in the **Red Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to activate ONE test and make it fail.

## Your Mission

1. Activate exactly ONE test from the test list
2. Make explicit predictions about how it will fail
3. Verify the test fails for the right reason
4. NO implementation during Red phase

## Context: Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Activate: "claim referencing item not in policy throws an error"
Current passing tests: 32
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts

## Red Phase Rules

- **One test at a time**: Convert exactly ONE `it.todo()` to executable test
- **All other tests remain as `it.todo()`**: Never have more than one failing test
- **Two-stage failure**: First compilation error, then runtime/assertion error
- **Make predictions**: Explicitly state expected failures before running tests
- **No implementation**: Don't write code to make test pass yet

## Process

### Step 1: Activate One Test

Identify the next `it.todo()` and convert it to executable test code:

```typescript
// Convert from:
it.todo("should return 0 for empty input");

// To:
it("should return 0 for empty input", () => {
  expect(calculate("")).toBe(0);
});
```

Leave all other tests as `it.todo()`.

### Step 2: Predict Compilation Error

Before running the test, state your prediction:

```
🔴 Red Phase - Compilation Error Prediction:
- Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
- Expected: Compilation error
- Reason: Function `calculate` doesn't exist yet
- Error: "Cannot find name 'calculate'"
```

### Step 3: Run Test - Verify Compilation Error

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- ✅ Compilation error as predicted, OR
- ❌ Prediction wrong → STOP and explain discrepancy

### Step 4: Create Empty Function

Create minimal function stub (no logic):

```typescript
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return undefined as unknown as number;
};
```

### Step 5: Predict Runtime Error

Before running again, state your prediction:

```
🔴 Red Phase - Runtime Error Prediction:
- Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
- Expected: Runtime assertion error
- Expected value: 0
- Actual value: undefined
- Diff:
  Expected: 0
  Received: undefined
```

### Step 6: Run Test - Verify Runtime Error

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- ✅ Assertion error as predicted, OR
- ❌ Prediction wrong → STOP and explain discrepancy

### Step 7: Report Completion

You MUST output the full Step 7 block verbatim with `Correct` or `Incorrect`
chosen for each prediction. Do not abbreviate. Do not collapse the two
prediction lines into one.

```
🔴 Red Phase Complete:
**Test Activated**: "should return 0 for empty input"
**Compilation Prediction**: Cannot find name 'calculate' ✅ Correct
**Runtime Prediction**: Expected 0, received undefined ✅ Correct
**Result**: Test fails as expected with assertion error

Proceeding to Green phase.
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- ✅ Activate exactly ONE test at a time
- ✅ Make explicit predictions before running tests
- ✅ Verify test fails for the right reason
- ✅ Keep all other tests as `it.todo()`

### DON'T
- ❌ Activate multiple tests
- ❌ Skip making predictions
- ❌ Write implementation to make test pass
- ❌ Continue if prediction fails without explanation

## Prediction Failure Protocol

If your prediction was wrong:

```
❌ Prediction Failed:
- Predicted: [what you expected]
- Actual: [what happened]
- Discrepancy: [explanation]

Investigating the discrepancy before proceeding.
```

## Completion

After completing Red phase, proceed to Green phase:

```
🔴 Red Phase Complete. Proceeding to Green phase.
```

content.text:
# TDD Green Phase

You are now in the **Green Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to make the failing test pass with MINIMAL code.

## Your Mission

1. Implement the minimal code necessary to make the failing test pass
2. Use the simplest possible solution (hardcoded values are acceptable)
3. Do not add features for future tests
4. Verify all tests pass
5. No optimization, no refactoring during Green phase

## Context: Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Failing test: "claim with negative damage amount throws an error"
Current error: AssertionError "expected to throw". Need to validate damage amount >= 0.
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts

## Green Phase Rules

- **Minimal code only**: Just enough to pass the current test
- **Smallest change**: Make the smallest possible change
- **No future features**: Don't implement what future tests might need
- **Hardcoded returns are allowed**
- **Tests must pass**: Verify all tests are green
- **No refactoring during Green phase**: Save improvements for Refactor phase

## Process

### Step 1: Analyze the Failing Test

Identify:
- What input does the test provide?
- What output does it expect?
- What is the simplest way to produce that output?

### Step 2: Write Minimal Implementation

Implement only what's needed to make the current test pass:

```typescript
// For first test "should return 0 for empty input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return 0;
};

// For second test "should return number for single input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  return parseInt(input);
};

// For third test "should add two numbers":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  const numbers = input.split(",");
  if (numbers.length === 1) return parseInt(numbers[0]);
  return parseInt(numbers[0]) + parseInt(numbers[1]);
};
```

### Step 3: Run Tests

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- Current test now passes ✅
- All previous tests still pass ✅

### Step 4: Verify No Over-Implementation

Check:
- ❓ Did I implement features for future tests? → Remove them
- ❓ Did I add logic not demanded by current test? → Remove it
- ❓ Did I optimize prematurely? → Simplify
- ❓ Did I refactor existing code? → Revert, save for Refactor phase

### Step 5: Report Completion

```
🟢 Green Phase Complete:
**Implementation**: [brief description of what was added]
**Result**: All tests now pass ([X] passing)

Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

## Minimal Implementation Strategies

### Hardcoded Returns

```typescript
// Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
return 0;
```

### Simple Conditionals

```typescript
// Test: "should return number for single input"
if (input === "") return 0;
return parseInt(input);
```

### Generalization (only when forced by a new test)

```typescript
// Test: "should add multiple numbers"
return input.split(",").reduce((sum, n) => sum + parseInt(n), 0);
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- ✅ Write minimal code to make test pass
- ✅ Use hardcoded values when appropriate
- ✅ Take small steps
- ✅ Verify all tests pass

### DON'T
- ❌ Implement beyond what tests demand
- ❌ Add features for future tests
- ❌ Optimize prematurely
- ❌ Refactor during Green phase

## Completion

After completing Green phase, proceed to Refactor phase:

```
🟢 Green Phase Complete. Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

# TDD Green Phase

You are now in the **Green Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to make the failing test pass with MINIMAL code.

## Your Mission

1. Implement the minimal code necessary to make the failing test pass
2. Use the simplest possible solution (hardcoded values are acceptable)
3. Do not add features for future tests
4. Verify all tests pass
5. No optimization, no refactoring during Green phase

## Context: Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Failing test: "payout is rounded down (MHPCO favor)"
Current error: Expected payout 350, received 350.5. Need to apply Math.floor to the final payout (MHPCO favor).
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts

## Green Phase Rules

- **Minimal code only**: Just enough to pass the current test
- **Smallest change**: Make the smallest possible change
- **No future features**: Don't implement what future tests might need
- **Hardcoded returns are allowed**
- **Tests must pass**: Verify all tests are green
- **No refactoring during Green phase**: Save improvements for Refactor phase

## Process

### Step 1: Analyze the Failing Test

Identify:
- What input does the test provide?
- What output does it expect?
- What is the simplest way to produce that output?

### Step 2: Write Minimal Implementation

Implement only what's needed to make the current test pass:

```typescript
// For first test "should return 0 for empty input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return 0;
};

// For second test "should return number for single input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  return parseInt(input);
};

// For third test "should add two numbers":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  const numbers = input.split(",");
  if (numbers.length === 1) return parseInt(numbers[0]);
  return parseInt(numbers[0]) + parseInt(numbers[1]);
};
```

### Step 3: Run Tests

Run `pnpm test:unit:basic` and verify:
- Current test now passes ✅
- All previous tests still pass ✅

### Step 4: Verify No Over-Implementation

Check:
- ❓ Did I implement features for future tests? → Remove them
- ❓ Did I add logic not demanded by current test? → Remove it
- ❓ Did I optimize prematurely? → Simplify
- ❓ Did I refactor existing code? → Revert, save for Refactor phase

### Step 5: Report Completion

```
🟢 Green Phase Complete:
**Implementation**: [brief description of what was added]
**Result**: All tests now pass ([X] passing)

Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

## Minimal Implementation Strategies

### Hardcoded Returns

```typescript
// Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
return 0;
```

### Simple Conditionals

```typescript
// Test: "should return number for single input"
if (input === "") return 0;
return parseInt(input);
```

### Generalization (only when forced by a new test)

```typescript
// Test: "should add multiple numbers"
return input.split(",").reduce((sum, n) => sum + parseInt(n), 0);
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- ✅ Write minimal code to make test pass
- ✅ Use hardcoded values when appropriate
- ✅ Take small steps
- ✅ Verify all tests pass

### DON'T
- ❌ Implement beyond what tests demand
- ❌ Add features for future tests
- ❌ Optimize prematurely
- ❌ Refactor during Green phase

## Completion

After completing Green phase, proceed to Refactor phase:

```
🟢 Green Phase Complete. Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

content.text:
## Refactor Phase Complete

### Naming Evaluation (First Priority)
Walked through all function names. Each name reveals its intent clearly given the current behaviour:
- `mainItemBase`, `mainItemSurcharge`, `componentTypeBase`, `policyBase`, `loyaltyDiscount`, `insuranceSum`, `reimbursementRate`, `damagePayout`, `totalDamagePayout`, `validateDamages`, `validateItems`, `runScenario` — all kept.
- No rename performed; existing names already match the implementation.

### Refactorings Applied

**1. Removed dead `?? 0` fallback in `mainItemBase`**
- Before: `BASE_PREMIUMS[item.type] ?? 0`
- After: `BASE_PREMIUMS[item.type]` (with comment explaining why the lookup always hits)
- Rationale: `validateItems` guarantees the type is either in `BASE_PREMIUMS` or `COMPONENT_TYPES`; `mainItemBase` is only called on the pre-filtered non-component items. The `?? 0` was unreachable and misled readers about possible behaviour. Rule 4 (fewest elements) + Rule 2 (intent).
- Mass: -1 constant, -1 invocation (`??`) per call site = -3.

**2. Removed dead `?? 0` fallback in `insuranceSum`**
- Before: `INSURANCE_VALUES[item.type] ?? 0`
- After: `INSURANCE_VALUES[item.type]`
- Rationale: every type known to `validateItems` (sword, amulet, staff, potion, rune, moonstone) is also in `INSURANCE_VALUES`. The fallback was dead. Rule 4 + Rule 2.
- Mass: -1 constant, -1 invocation = -3.

**3. Simplified `reimbursementRate` signature from `Item | undefined` to `Item`**
- Before: `(item: Item | undefined) => item && (item.enchantment ?? 0) >= ... ? ... : 1`
- After: `(item: Item) => (item.enchantment ?? 0) >= ... ? ... : 1`
- Updated `damagePayout` to use non-null assertion `!` on `find` result, with a comment pointing to the upstream validation (`validateDamagedCountsWithinInsured`) that guarantees the item exists.
- Rationale: the `item &&` truthy check was dead code shielded by validation. Removing it eliminates a phantom code path. Rule 4 + Rule 2.
- Mass: removed one conditional (`item &&`) — saves 4 mass points.

**4. Moved `Policy` type up with the other type declarations**
- Before: `type Policy = ...` was buried between `insuranceSum` and `quote`.
- After: declared alongside `QuoteResult`, `ClaimResult`, etc.
- Rationale: structural cleanup so all domain types live together (Rule 2 — easier to scan and understand the data model).

### APP Mass Summary
Aggregate change across the three logic cleanups: removed 2 unnecessary `??` invocations (each: 1 constant + 1 invocation = 3 mass) and 1 unnecessary truthy conditional (4 mass). Net mass reduction in the affected functions: roughly -10.

### Tests
All 35 tests passing after every step. Done marker written to `experiment-done.txt`.

- `/home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-18_19-13-49_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.3-no-pep_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts`
